entities.name: śabda
entities.cite: when a word is employed with the intention of another word

entities.name: artha
entities.cite: that uttered word is not the expressor of meaning

entities.name: aviccheda
entities.cite: both lines, sadhu and apabhramsa, sista-transmission and vernacular transmission, are *unbroken*

axioms.name: aviccheda
axioms.cite: both lines, sadhu and apabhramsa, sista-transmission and vernacular transmission, are *unbroken*

axioms.name: na_abhidhayaka
axioms.cite: that uttered word is not the expressor; the intended word is, through it

denials.name: abhidhayati
denials.cite: that uttered word is not the expressor

claims.name: niyantrita
claims.cite: when a word is employed with the intention of another word

claims.name: na_abhidhayaka
claims.cite: that word is not the expressor of the meaning

rejected_readings.label: employed_word_expresses
rejected_readings.rendering: When a word is employed with the intention of another word, that employed word expresses the meaning directly.
rejected_readings.why: Directly contradicts the verse's explicit claim that the employed word is not the expressor. Confuses the conduit (employed form) with the actual expressor (intended word governed by vivaksa).

rejected_readings.label: apabhramsa_only_scope
rejected_readings.rendering: Apabhramsa vernacular forms alone are not expressors of Sanskrit meanings; only Sanskrit forms express.
rejected_readings.why: Narrows the verse's universal principle (any word employed with intention of another) to a specific case, missing the verse's indifference to which stream is 'first' (gavi/gauh either direction). The commentary makes clear the doctrine applies equally whether sadhu reaches through apabhramsa or vice versa.

rejected_readings.label: vivaksa_irrelevant
rejected_readings.rendering: Both the employed word and the intended word equally express the meaning regardless of intention.
rejected_readings.why: Eliminates the role of vivaksa (intention) that the verse places at the center of the determination. The whole point is that expression is *determined by* which form the vivaksa deploys, not independent of it.

notes: The verse resolves the Brahmakanda's standoff (1.153–154) between two equally continuous transmission lines by introducing intention (vivaksa) as the criterion for expression. Contested: whether this generalizes beyond apabhramsa to all cases of one word reaching through another (citation, metalepsis, even dhvani-sphota); the kanda-closing position and the commentary's 'employment-through' locution favor the broad reading, encoding that here. Ontological strain: 'expressor' (abhidhayaka) is modeled as a relation rather than an independent entity, reflecting its dependency on vivaksa.
